charts.description: Glycolysis: glucose is invested with two ATP and committed at phosphofructokinase-1, split into triose phosphates, then oxidized to yield NADH and a net two ATP and two pyruvate, with PFK-1 allosterically gated by the cell's energy charge.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — Glycolysis Is an Energy-Conversion Pathway
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: Rethinking glycolysis: on the biochemical logic of metabolic pathways
sources.authors: Bar-Even, A.; Flamholz, A.; Noor, E.; Milo, R.

sources.title: Aerobic glycolysis: meeting the metabolic requirements of cell proliferation
sources.authors: Lunt, S. Y.; Vander Heiden, M. G.
sources.journal: Annual Review of Cell and Developmental Biology

charts.description: Gluconeogenesis: pyruvate is converted to glucose by bypassing the three irreversible glycolytic steps (pyruvate carboxylase/PEPCK, fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, glucose-6-phosphatase), gated by energy charge and reciprocal hormonal regulation in liver.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — Gluconeogenesis
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: Molecular physiology of the regulation of hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycolysis
sources.authors: Pilkis, S. J.; Granner, D. K.

sources.title: Gluconeogenesis and related aspects of glycolysis
sources.authors: Hers, H. G.; Hue, L.

nodes.label: Energy charge adequate?
nodes.detail: ATP and substrate availability gate flux

charts.description: Citric acid cycle: acetyl-CoA condenses with oxaloacetate and is oxidized through eight steps that release two CO2, three NADH, one FADH2 and one GTP per turn, regenerating oxaloacetate, with isocitrate dehydrogenase gated by the NADH/ATP level.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — The Citric Acid Cycle
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: The key role of anaplerosis and cataplerosis for citric acid cycle function
sources.authors: Owen, O. E.; Kalhan, S. C.; Hanson, R. W.

sources.title: The role of citric acid in intermediate metabolism in animal tissues
sources.authors: Krebs, H. A.; Johnson, W. A.

charts.description: Oxidative phosphorylation: electrons from NADH/FADH2 pass through respiratory complexes to O2 while protons are pumped across the inner membrane, and the resulting proton-motive force drives ATP synthase, with respiratory control by ADP availability.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — Oxidative Phosphorylation
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: Coupling of phosphorylation to electron and hydrogen transfer by a chemi-osmotic type of mechanism
sources.authors: Mitchell, P.

sources.title: Oxidative phosphorylation at the fin de siècle
sources.authors: Saraste, M.

nodes.label: Protons pumped out
nodes.detail: H+ moved at I, III, IV

charts.description: Pentose phosphate pathway: glucose-6-phosphate enters the oxidative branch (G6PD, rate-limiting) to generate NADPH and ribose precursors, and the non-oxidative branch interconverts sugars to balance NADPH versus ribose-5-phosphate demand.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — The Pentose Phosphate Pathway
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: The return of metabolism: biochemistry and physiology of the pentose phosphate pathway
sources.authors: Stincone, A.; et al.

sources.title: The pentose phosphate pathway and cancer
sources.authors: Patra, K. C.; Hay, N.

charts.description: Fatty acid synthesis: cytosolic acetyl-CoA is carboxylated to malonyl-CoA (ACC, committed step), and fatty acid synthase iterates condensation-reduction-dehydration-reduction cycles on ACP, adding two carbons per round until palmitate is released.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — Fatty Acid Metabolism
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: Fatty acid synthesis and its regulation
sources.authors: Wakil, S. J.; Stoops, J. K.; Joshi, V. C.

sources.title: The crystal structure of a mammalian fatty acid synthase
sources.authors: Maier, T.; Leibundgut, M.; Ban, N.

charts.description: Fatty acid beta-oxidation: activated fatty acyl-CoA is shuttled into the mitochondrion by carnitine, then each cycle of oxidation, hydration, oxidation and thiolytic cleavage shortens the chain by two carbons, yielding acetyl-CoA, FADH2 and NADH.

sources.title: A general introduction to the biochemistry of mitochondrial fatty acid β-oxidation
sources.authors: Houten, S. M.; Wanders, R. J.

sources.title: Mammalian mitochondrial beta-oxidation
sources.authors: Eaton, S.; Bartlett, K.; Pourfarzam, M.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — Fatty Acids Are Oxidized in Mitochondria
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

charts.description: Fermentation: when oxygen is limiting, cells regenerate NAD+ to sustain glycolysis by reducing pyruvate, either to lactate via lactate dehydrogenase (animals) or to ethanol via pyruvate decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase (yeast).

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — Fermentations Provide a Means of Oxidizing NADH
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: Evolution of carbohydrate metabolic pathways
sources.authors: Romano, A. H.; Conway, T.

sources.title: An evolutionary perspective on the Crabtree effect
sources.authors: Pfeiffer, T.; Morley, A.

charts.description: Urea cycle: ammonia and bicarbonate are condensed (CPS1, allosterically activated by N-acetylglutamate) into carbamoyl phosphate, then through citrulline, argininosuccinate and arginine to release urea while regenerating ornithine for the next turn.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — The Urea Cycle
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: Regulation of enzymes of the urea cycle and arginine metabolism
sources.authors: Morris, S. M. Jr.

sources.title: Suggested guidelines for the diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders
sources.authors: Häberle, J.; et al.

charts.description: Calvin cycle: RuBisCO fixes CO2 onto ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate, the 3-phosphoglycerate product is reduced using ATP and NADPH to G3P, and most G3P is used to regenerate RuBP while a fraction is exported to make sugars.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — The Calvin Cycle
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: The path of carbon in photosynthesis
sources.authors: Bassham, J. A.; Benson, A. A.; Calvin, M.

sources.title: The Calvin cycle revisited
sources.authors: Raines, C. A.

charts.description: Light reactions: photons excite Photosystem II, water is split to replace electrons and release O2, electrons travel the Z-scheme through cytochrome b6f and Photosystem I to make NADPH, and the proton gradient drives ATP synthesis.

sources.title: Biochemistry, 5th ed. — The Light Reactions of Photosynthesis
sources.authors: Berg, J. M.; Tymoczko, J. L.; Stryer, L.

sources.title: Structure and function of photosystems I and II
sources.authors: Nelson, N.; Yocum, C. F.

sources.title: Photosynthetic energy conversion: natural and artificial
sources.authors: Barber, J.

charts.description: Biological nitrogen fixation: the nitrogenase Fe protein uses ATP to deliver electrons one at a time to the MoFe protein's FeMo-cofactor, which accumulates enough electrons and protons to reduce inert N2 to two ammonia, later assimilated by GS/GOGAT.

sources.title: Mechanism of nitrogen fixation by nitrogenase: the next stage
sources.authors: Hoffman, B. M.; Lukoyanov, D.; Yang, Z. Y.; Dean, D. R.; Seefeldt, L. C.

sources.title: Mechanism of Mo-dependent nitrogenase
sources.authors: Seefeldt, L. C.; Hoffman, B. M.; Dean, D. R.

sources.title: Nitrogenases
sources.authors: Burris, R. H.

charts.description: Apoptosis: extrinsic death receptors activate caspase-8 while intrinsic stress is gated by the BCL-2 family at the mitochondrion; cytochrome c release forms the apoptosome and activates caspase-9, converging on executioner caspases that dismantle the cell.

sources.title: Apoptosis: controlled demolition at the cellular level
sources.authors: Taylor, R. C.; Cullen, S. P.; Martin, S. J.

sources.title: Cell Death Signaling
sources.authors: Green, D. R.; Llambi, F.
sources.journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Biology

sources.title: Molecular mechanisms of caspase regulation during apoptosis
sources.authors: Riedl, S. J.; Shi, Y.

charts.description: Necroptosis: when caspase-8 is inhibited downstream of death receptors, RIPK1 and RIPK3 assemble the necrosome, RIPK3 phosphorylates MLKL, and oligomerized MLKL permeabilizes the plasma membrane to cause lytic, inflammatory cell death.

sources.title: Necroptosis and its role in inflammation
sources.authors: Pasparakis, M.; Vandenabeele, P.

sources.title: Mixed lineage kinase domain-like protein mediates necrosis signaling downstream of RIP3 kinase
sources.authors: Sun, L.; et al.

sources.title: Phosphorylation-driven assembly of the RIP1-RIP3 complex regulates programmed necrosis
sources.authors: Cho, Y. S.; et al.

charts.description: Cell cycle: cyclin-CDK complexes drive progression through G1, S, G2 and M, with the G1/S restriction point, the G2/M DNA-integrity checkpoint and the spindle-assembly checkpoint arresting the cycle for repair when conditions are not met.

sources.title: Cyclin-dependent kinases: engines, clocks, and microprocessors
sources.authors: Morgan, D. O.
sources.journal: Annual Review of Cell and Developmental Biology

sources.title: Cell cycle, CDKs and cancer: a changing paradigm
sources.authors: Malumbres, M.; Barbacid, M.

sources.title: Checkpoints: controls that ensure the order of cell cycle events
sources.authors: Hartwell, L. H.; Weinert, T. A.

charts.description: GPCR signaling: ligand binding makes the receptor a GEF that loads GTP onto Galpha, the dissociated subunits activate effectors producing second messengers and a cellular response, and GTP hydrolysis plus GRK/arrestin desensitization reset the system.

sources.title: Seven-transmembrane receptors
sources.authors: Pierce, K. L.; Premont, R. T.; Lefkowitz, R. J.

sources.title: G proteins: transducers of receptor-generated signals
sources.authors: Gilman, A. G.

sources.title: Crystal structure of the β2 adrenergic receptor-Gs protein complex
sources.authors: Rasmussen, S. G.; et al.

charts.description: Insulin signaling: the insulin receptor autophosphorylates and recruits IRS proteins, activating PI3K-Akt to branch into GLUT4-mediated glucose uptake, glycogen synthesis and mTOR-driven growth, with S6K feedback dampening IRS.

sources.title: Insulin signalling and the regulation of glucose and lipid metabolism
sources.authors: Saltiel, A. R.; Kahn, C. R.

sources.title: Critical nodes in signalling pathways: insights into insulin action
sources.authors: Taniguchi, C. M.; Emanuelli, B.; Kahn, C. R.

sources.title: Insulin signaling in health and disease
sources.authors: White, M. F.

charts.description: JAK-STAT signaling: cytokine-induced receptor dimerization activates associated JAK kinases that phosphorylate the receptor and recruited STATs, which dimerize, enter the nucleus and drive transcription, with SOCS providing negative feedback.

sources.title: Jak-STAT pathways and transcriptional activation in response to IFNs and other extracellular signaling proteins
sources.authors: Darnell, J. E. Jr.; Kerr, I. M.; Stark, G. R.

sources.title: The JAK-STAT pathway at twenty
sources.authors: Stark, G. R.; Darnell, J. E. Jr.

sources.title: JAK-STAT signaling: from interferons to cytokines
sources.authors: Schindler, C.; Levy, D. E.; Decker, T.

charts.description: MAPK cascade: an activated receptor tyrosine kinase recruits Grb2/SOS to load Ras-GTP, which triggers the three-tier Raf-MEK-ERK kinase cascade; active ERK enters the nucleus to drive proliferation, with phosphatase feedback.

sources.title: The MAPK signaling cascade
sources.authors: Seger, R.; Krebs, E. G.

sources.title: Mammalian MAP kinase signalling cascades
sources.authors: Chang, L.; Karin, M.

sources.title: MAP kinase pathways
sources.authors: Morrison, D. K.
sources.journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Biology

charts.description: NF-kB signaling: inflammatory stimuli activate the IKK complex, which phosphorylates IkB to trigger its ubiquitination and degradation; freed NF-kB enters the nucleus to drive inflammatory genes, including newly synthesized IkB that provides negative feedback.

sources.title: Shared principles in NF-κB signaling
sources.authors: Hayden, M. S.; Ghosh, S.

sources.title: Phosphorylation meets ubiquitination: the control of NF-κB activity
sources.authors: Karin, M.; Ben-Neriah, Y.

sources.title: The NF-κB family of transcription factors and its regulation
sources.authors: Oeckinghaus, A.; Ghosh, S.
sources.journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Biology

charts.description: Notch signaling: a Delta/Jagged ligand on a neighboring cell engages Notch and the pulling force exposes cleavage sites; ADAM and gamma-secretase release the Notch intracellular domain, which enters the nucleus with CSL/Mastermind to activate HES/HEY genes.

sources.title: The canonical Notch signaling pathway: unfolding the activation mechanism
sources.authors: Kopan, R.; Ilagan, M. X.

sources.title: Notch signalling: a simple pathway becomes complex
sources.authors: Bray, S. J.

sources.title: Notch signalling in context
sources.authors: Bray, S. J.

charts.description: p53 DNA-damage response: ATM/ATR phosphorylate p53, disrupting MDM2 binding so p53 accumulates and induces p21-mediated arrest and repair; if damage is irreparable, p53 drives apoptosis, while re-synthesized MDM2 provides negative feedback.

sources.title: p53 in health and disease
sources.authors: Vousden, K. H.; Lane, D. P.

sources.title: Regulation of p53 in response to DNA damage
sources.authors: Lakin, N. D.; Jackson, S. P.

sources.title: Modes of p53 regulation
sources.authors: Kruse, J. P.; Gu, W.

charts.description: Wnt/beta-catenin signaling: without Wnt, the Axin/APC/GSK3 destruction complex phosphorylates beta-catenin for degradation; Wnt binding to Frizzled/LRP recruits Dishevelled, inhibits the complex, and lets beta-catenin accumulate and activate TCF/LEF target genes.

sources.title: Wnt/β-catenin signaling: components, mechanisms, and diseases
sources.authors: MacDonald, B. T.; Tamai, K.; He, X.

sources.title: Wnt/β-catenin signaling and disease
sources.authors: Clevers, H.; Nusse, R.

sources.title: Wnt/β-Catenin Signaling, Disease, and Emerging Therapeutic Modalities
sources.authors: Nusse, R.; Clevers, H.

charts.description: Domains of life: comparison of small-subunit rRNA sequences resolves cellular life into three domains - Bacteria, Archaea and Eukarya - each with characteristic example lineages, with eukaryotes arising from within the archaeal radiation.

sources.title: Towards a natural system of organisms: proposal for the domains Archaea, Bacteria, and Eucarya
sources.authors: Woese, C. R.; Kandler, O.; Wheelis, M. L.

sources.title: Phylogenetic structure of the prokaryotic domain: the primary kingdoms
sources.authors: Woese, C. R.; Fox, G. E.

sources.title: A new view of the tree of life
sources.authors: Hug, L. A.; et al.
